nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving shallow streams, flooded forest margins, and lagoons with dense leaf litter, submerged roots, and overhanging vegetation; water often stained dark brown with tannins

decorPreferences: coconut cave or ceramic cave for spawning site
dense planting (Amazon swords, Java fern, Vallisneria, floating plants for shade)
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves for tannins and shelter)
low ambient light or shaded areas

diet.notes: The Three-Striped Apisto is a carnivore that feeds primarily on small invertebrates. Live or frozen brine shrimp (Artemia), daphnia, cyclops, micro-worms, and bloodworms are all accepted readily. Most individuals will gradually take high-quality frozen and freeze-dried equivalents, and established fish will often accept micro-pellets and small cichlid pellets. Offering a varied diet is especially important when conditioning a breeding pair, and live foods are strongly recommended to trigger spawning.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, cardinal tetra, neon tetra)
small corydoras catfish (Corydoras habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (territory and hybridisation risk)
boisterous fast-moving fish that stress the pair
large plecos that disrupt cave sites

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by OpenCage (oencage), 2017x2020. Sharp, vibrant male in full colour: bold black lateral stripe, long filamentous dorsal-ray and caudal extensions clearly shown — the key diagnostic feature. Whole fish in frame.

Photo by Mazapura, 800x600. Male flaring at a mirror, showing the metallic blue-green flanks and raised dorsal fin described in the summary. Slight aquarium colour cast and mirror edge in frame; kept as a colour-variety extra.

Photo by Douglas Lopes, 2048x1147. Clean full-profile side view on dark ground clearly showing the black lateral stripe and body form; colours are muted (subdued individual) so kept as a non-recommended reference angle.

summary: The Three-Striped Apisto is a South American dwarf cichlid from the Paraguay basin and Pantanal. Males develop vivid blue-green flanks and dramatically elongated dorsal fin rays. Among Apistogramma, it is comparatively tolerant of neutral, moderately hard water. Naturally harem-polygynous, with female-led cave brood care.
careNotes:
Apistogramma trifasciata belongs to the trifasciata species group and is among the more adaptable members of the genus when it comes to water chemistry. It can tolerate a pH up to approximately 7.5 and moderate hardness up to around 12 gH, which means it can be kept without reverse-osmosis water — though softer, slightly acidic conditions (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 2–8) are preferred for long-term health and breeding. Native to the Paraguay River drainage including the Pantanal wetlands of Brazil, Bolivia, and Paraguay, as well as the middle Amazon fringe, it inhabits shallow, slow-moving or still waters rich in leaf litter, submerged roots, and heavy overhead shading.

Males are noticeably larger than females and display vivid metallic blue-green flanks, a bold black lateral stripe, and characteristically long, filamentous extensions on the dorsal fin rays. This elongated dorsal fin distinguishes A. trifasciata from many related species and makes males identifiable even at a distance. Females are smaller and yellower, turning bright lemon-yellow during brood care — a reliable indicator that active breeding is underway.

A minimum 60 L tank with a fine sandy substrate, multiple caves (coconut shells, ceramic pots, or inverted flowerpots), leaf litter, and dense planting provides the structure these fish need. Subdued lighting helps reduce stress. In harem setups — one male kept with two to three females — a larger tank of 100 L or more with broken sightlines between female territories is recommended. Water changes of 20–30% weekly maintain quality; warm, stale water and elevated nitrates tend to depress immune function and inhibit spawning. A temperature around 26–28°C is beneficial when conditioning fish for breeding. This species is sensitive to declining water quality but is generally less demanding about low pH than strict blackwater Apistogramma.
breedingNotes: The Three-Striped Apisto spawns in caves, with the female taking exclusive responsibility for egg guarding and fry care while the male defends the outer territory perimeter. Clutch size is typically 50–120 eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C, and fry are free-swimming roughly 5–7 days later. The female may move fry between pits in the substrate and will actively defend them against tankmates and even the male. First foods should be freshly hatched Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, and infusoria, with commercially available fry food offered as a supplement. The male can be removed after spawning if aggression toward the female is observed, though in spacious tanks with visual barriers the male tends not to disrupt the female's care. Soft, acidic water generally improves fertilisation rates and fry survival; conditioning the pair with live foods and raising the temperature by 1–2°C often triggers spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma trifasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Paraguay, middle Amazon, Pantanal), water parameters (pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm / ~1–10 dH, temperature 22–27°C), adult size (males to ~7 cm, females ~3.5 cm), diet (small invertebrates), tank setup, behaviour, and breeding notes. Cross-checked against FishBase and Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma trifasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (max ~7 cm TL for males), native range (Paraguay and lower Amazon basins, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Argentina), temperature range (22–28°C), and diet (carnivore — small invertebrates). Confirmed trifasciata group membership.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry including harem setup guidance, cave requirements, diet (live/frozen invertebrates), water parameters, and recommended tank mates. Noted A. trifasciata as one of the hardier species tolerating slightly harder and more neutral water.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species guide
additional.notes: Confirmed species group placement (trifasciata group), elongated dorsal fin as a key male identification feature, female yellow colouration during brood care, female-led maternal mouthbrooding-adjacent cave care, and harem polygyny in the wild. Recommended sand substrate and leaf litter.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Apistogramma trifasciata species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for breeding detail: clutch size 50–120 eggs, egg incubation ~48–72 hours at 26°C, fry free-swimming at day 5–7, female-exclusive brood care noted. Also confirmed male fin morphology (elongated dorsal fin filaments distinguishing trifasciata from other Apistogramma).

fish.breedingNotes: The Three-Striped Apisto is a cave spawner. The female guards the eggs and leads fry care exclusively while the male defends the outer territory. Clutch size is typically 50–120 eggs, with fry becoming free-swimming around day 5–7. Soft, acidic water and conditioning with live foods are recommended to trigger spawning. The male may be separated if he harasses the female after the spawn.
